childCommentText: @something.elsa No, but Congress had enough lawmakers at the time to pass the Immigration and Naturalization Act of 1952, which is now part of the justification being used to deport many of the people who have been picked up today. People were imprisoned and deported during the McCarthy era. Meanwhile, here at home, Jim Crow was raging across the South, the American justice system was still turning a blind eye to the several Black people a year being lynched, federal agencies were burgling and wiretapping nearly every left-wing organization in the country (efforts the government maintained well through the 70s), firing federal employees and imprisoning people under "emergency laws" that allowed them to skirt due process, and so on and so on and so on. And never forget that decades after the McCarthy era, Philadelphia bombed its own citizens to "take out" the Black libertarian organization MOVE. No one was ever held criminally responsible. As I said above, people need to discern whether they don't like the message (which is also being stated by older Black people like my aunts and uncles who are in their late 70s and 80s and lived everything I listed, Black thought leaders and more) or if they don't like the messenger.

childCommentText: @nicolas.anthony unfortunately that demand really could only be placed on large, publicly traded, chain conglomerates and ultra high end dining. Because our local small owned businesses do need tipping to become successful enough to pay themselves and their workers decent money. Without investors you cant float high wages to your staff until the business picks up. I am all for changing the way that publicly traded companies operate and for demanding that small high end successful businesses pay for things like health insurance. But we dont get there by attacking the very concept of tipping. Tipping is appreciated and common elsewhere but doesnt feel like such a requirement. Its not like there is or should ever be a need to abolish the entire custom. And personally withdrawing your support from the customer by not tipping, only harms other workers and your own service experience.

childCommentText: First and foremost as someone who works in a tipped environment, I will be the first to say it does has benefits but more cons for sure. Do I agree that employers should pay wages? But I work very hard, I have several certifications and I work at bars that serve to a clientele that understands the tipping culture 98% of the time. Now. childCommentText: @jzels What’s surprising is that folks think Trump came up with the idea of having rules against racism. Republicans are wrong, withholding research funding has nothing to do with enforcing the Civil Rights Act 1964. Harvard University is subject to federal regulations against racial discrimination, primarily through Title VI of the Civil Rights Act of 1964. As a recipient of federal funds, Harvard must ensure that its programs and activities do not discriminate on the basis of race, color, or national origin. This includes not excluding individuals from participation or denying them benefits based on these factors. While Harvard is not a public institution and therefore not directly subject to the Fourteenth Amendment's Equal Protection Clause, the Supreme Court has held that Title VI's protections are essentially the same as those provided by the Constitution.

childCommentText:
@a_woodnymph_ 💔Devastating. The injustice and inequity couldn’t be more stark. Countless lives and homes destroyed by fossil fuelled climate chaos while a few billionaires fund ultra-polluting space joyrides for the elite.

When you live in a bubble of private jets and rocket ships, it’s easy to forget that most people are just trying to keep the lights on and food on the table — we’re dealing with the mess that their greed and excessive pollution creates.

If billionaires paid a little more in taxes (it’s just a drop in the ocean for them), that money could fund healthcare, education, fair wages, and greater protection from the climate crisis. 

Let’s urge governments to tax the super-rich now and use those funds to support people and protect the planet, for us all.

parentText: This is absolute nonsense. Salt from any source is NOT a super food. It is over consumed by most Americans and this contributes to hypertension and mortality (decreases lifespan). childCommentText: Men have no way of lowering their Iron Accumulation besides Bleeding, so the best bet for them is to donate. Women subtly lower/regulate their iron levels through menstruation which is also why Women who go through menopause end up having more issues with CVD or C4ncer/disease. Donating is a way to keeping your iron levels very low to prevent Excess Iron build which ages you and progresses c4ncer. Most Iron accumulation is in the form of Iron Oxide, and since it’s called “Oxidative Stress” its literally your bones, tissues and cells “Rusting” (Iron Oxide is rust) from the inside out. People think Anemia is caused by low Iron, but Anemia is caused by Low Copper/Iron Dysregulation. Copper is needed to regulate and make RBCs from Iron, it also recycles Iron constantly and keeps it flowing in your blood rather than your tissues, but most people are deficient. Thats why in the USA so many people have “Anemia” even if they are overdosing themselves with Iron pills.

childCommentText:
@iam_nice_ceo 1. To reduce tightness and pressure: Modern football socks, especially from big brands like Nike or Adidas, are designed to be tight to support blood flow. But some players feel they’re too tight, which can cause discomfort, numbness, or even pain. Cutting the socks helps relieve that pressure.
2. To prevent muscle cramps: Some players believe that overly tight socks contribute to muscle cramps during matches. Loosening the grip by cutting the socks allows muscles to move and breathe more freely.
3. Personal comfort: It can simply come down to personal preference. Some players just feel more comfortable playing with modified socks.
4. To wear “grip socks” underneath: Many modern players wear special grip socks that help their feet stay firmly in place inside the boots. To follow uniform rules, they cut the official team socks and wear them over the grip socks.
